hero-lede: There's an old idea that a tool is meant to make a person better at something — not to do the thing in their place. A hammer doesn't build the house for you; it lets your arm do more. Augmented intelligence asks the same question of a computer: could it help you think further, while you stay the one who decides? Here's where the idea came from, and what it might mean for you.

There's an old idea that a tool should make a person more able, not stand in for them. Augmented intelligence asks that of AI: can it help you think further while you keep deciding? Here's where it comes from, and why it might matter to you.

This whole site rests on one old idea: a tool should raise what a person can do, not replace them. Augmented intelligence applies that to AI — a thinking partner that extends your reach while you keep the final call. Here's where it comes from, and why it might matter to you.

One old idea runs under this whole site: tools should raise human ability, not replace it. Augmented intelligence is that idea applied to AI — extend your reach, keep your judgement. Here's its origin and its stakes.
Augmented intelligence: AI that amplifies human judgement rather than replacing it — the idea this site is built on. Its origin, and what's at stake for you.
where-it-comes-from: Back in 1962, a thinker named Douglas Engelbart wrote down a plain hope: that machines might help people think better — help us work through hard problems we couldn't crack on our own. He wasn't dreaming of machines that took our place. He was dreaming of machines that lifted what a person could manage, the way a ladder helps you reach a high shelf. He called this augmenting human intellect. The idea is much older than today's AI, and it still shapes how some people choose to use it.

In 1962, Douglas Engelbart wrote a paper with a plain hope behind it: that machines could help people think better, and work through problems too hard to crack alone. He didn't want machines that replaced people — he wanted machines that lifted what a person could do. He called it augmenting human intellect. The idea is older than today's AI, and still guides how many people use it.

The phrase traces back to Douglas Engelbart, who in 1962 wrote a paper titled Augmenting Human Intellect. His aim wasn't machines that replace people, but machines that raise what a person can do — tools to help us tackle problems too tangled to manage alone. It's an old idea, and it still guides how many people approach AI today.

The phrase goes back to Douglas Engelbart's 1962 paper Augmenting Human Intellect: not machines that replace people, but machines that raise what a person can do against problems too tangled to manage alone. Older than today's AI — and still a live way to frame it.
The phrase is Douglas Engelbart's, from his 1962 paper Augmenting Human Intellect: machines built to raise human capability, not stand in for it. Decades older than current AI, still a live framing.
augmentation-vs-automation: There are two different things a machine can try to do, and they're worth keeping apart. One is automation: the machine does the whole task instead of you, so you can step back and leave it to run. The other is augmentation: the machine makes you more able — it carries some of the load, but you stay in the middle, looking and choosing. Picture a calculator you punch numbers into and check, versus a vending machine that just hands you a snack. Both can be handy. They simply aren't the same, and which one a tool is built for changes who's really in charge.

A machine can try two different things. Automation means it does the task instead of you, so you step back. Augmentation means it makes you more able — it shares the load, but you stay in the middle, looking and choosing. Both can be useful. The difference is where you end up: out of the loop, or still holding the wheel.

Two paths sit side by side. Automation aims to do the task instead of you, so you can step away. Augmentation aims to make you more capable — it shares the load, but you stay central, watching and deciding. Both have their place. The difference is simply where you land: out of the loop, or still holding the wheel.

Two aims sit side by side. Automation: the task done instead of you, so you step away. Augmentation: you made more capable, central, still deciding. Both have uses — the split is where you land, out of the loop or holding the wheel.
Automation does the task in your place; augmentation makes you more capable while you stay central and deciding. Both have uses — the split is whether you leave the loop or keep the wheel.
why-it-matters: You've probably heard the worry: AI will replace you. Augmentation offers a quieter way to look at it. If you treat AI as a thinking partner, you can get more reach and move faster — but the weighing, the deciding, the final call all stay with you. You don't hand those over. A tool can only help while you keep paying attention, though; lean on it without looking and it stops augmenting and starts deciding for you. Knowing what it's actually good at can help you keep that line clear. Whether the trade feels right is yours to weigh.

You've probably heard that AI will replace you. Augmentation is a calmer answer. As a thinking partner, AI can give you more reach and speed — but the judgement and the final call stay yours. You don't hand those over. That holds only while you stay attentive: lean on it blindly and it slips from helping into deciding for you. Whether the trade is worth it is yours to weigh.

Used as a thinking partner, AI can give you more reach and speed — but the judgement, and the final call, stay yours. You never hand those over. That only holds while you stay attentive, though: lean on it blindly and it quietly slips from helping into deciding. Whether the trade is worth it is yours to weigh.

Against AI will replace you, augmentation is a steadier answer: more reach and speed, judgement still yours, the final call never handed over. But it holds only while you stay attentive — lean blindly and it slips from helping into deciding. Whether that trade suits you is yours to weigh.
